seed_prompt: A playful hand-drawn technical comic, 4 panels, teaching one idea: without WAL, a new write disappears in a crash; with WAL, the write survives because it was written down first. Use the same cartoon style, line quality, panel feel, coloring, and professor shiba character style as the reference image. Cast: professor inu shiba dog, RAM, disk-tree. Panel 1: without WAL, RAM says 'I have it!' while disk-tree still has old data. Panel 2: crash hits, RAM disappears, disk-tree says 'I only know the old version'. Panel 3: professor shiba explains 'Write is appended to log first' and shows new write -> WAL -> RAM. Panel 4: after restart, professor shiba points to WAL and disk-tree now has the new write. Keep it fun, hand-drawn, and clear. Very few words.
draft_prompt: A four-panel hand-drawn technical comic strip in a playful, sketchy cartoon style with visible colored pencil and watercolor textures on textured paper. The layout is a 2x2 grid with thick, wobbly panel borders. Panel 1 (Top Left, labeled 'WITHOUT WAL: THE RISK'): An anthropomorphic, bubbly glowing RAM character excitedly holds a shiny blue block labeled 'NEW DATA!' saying in a speech bubble, 'I have it!'. Beside it, a stoic, rooted Disk-tree character (a tree with hard drives for leaves) holds a dusty brown block labeled 'OLD DATA'. Panel 2 (Top Right, labeled 'CRASH!'): A jagged red explosion icon with the text 'CRASH!' dominates. The RAM character has vanished into a puff of smoke. The Disk-tree character looks confused, still holding the dusty 'OLD DATA' block, with a speech bubble saying, 'I only know the old version.' A broken power plug icon is in the corner. Panel 3 (Bottom Left, labeled 'WITH WAL: THE SOLUTION'): Professor Inu Shiba (a cute Shiba Inu dog wearing a tweed jacket and glasses) stands next to a chalkboard diagram. He points with a stick to a flow: A 'NEW WRITE' arrow points to a durable-looking, unrolling scroll labeled 'WAL (Log)', and then an arrow points from the WAL to the bubbly RAM character. The Professor says, 'Write is appended to log first.' Panel 4 (Bottom Right, labeled 'AFTER RESTART'): The scene is calm with a 'RESTART' icon (a circular arrow). Professor Inu Shiba smiles and points to the open WAL scroll which shows the blue 'NEW DATA!' block recorded on it. The Disk-tree character is now happily holding a shiny blue 'NEW DATA!' block, recovered from the log.

draft_payload.draft_prompt: A four-panel hand-drawn technical comic strip in a playful, sketchy cartoon style with visible colored pencil and watercolor textures on textured paper. The layout is a 2x2 grid with thick, wobbly panel borders. Panel 1 (Top Left, labeled 'WITHOUT WAL: THE RISK'): An anthropomorphic, bubbly glowing RAM character excitedly holds a shiny blue block labeled 'NEW DATA!' saying in a speech bubble, 'I have it!'. Beside it, a stoic, rooted Disk-tree character (a tree with hard drives for leaves) holds a dusty brown block labeled 'OLD DATA'. Panel 2 (Top Right, labeled 'CRASH!'): A jagged red explosion icon with the text 'CRASH!' dominates. The RAM character has vanished into a puff of smoke. Professor Inu Shiba smiles and points to the open WAL scroll which shows the blue 'NEW DATA!' block recorded on it. The Disk-tree character is now happily holding a shiny blue 'NEW DATA!' block, recovered from the log.
draft_payload.story_purpose: To visually explain the concept of Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) in database systems, demonstrating how writing data to a persistent log *before* applying it to volatile memory ensures data durability and survival in the event of a system crash.
key_elements: WAL (Write-Ahead Log) represented as a durable scroll
labels_icons: Icons: Jagged explosion 'CRASH!', broken power plug, circular arrow 'RESTART'
draft_payload.style_notes: Consistent hand-drawn sketch style with visible pencil lines and crayon/watercolor fill textures. The color palette is warm and inviting but uses distinct colors for data states (bright blue for new data, dusty brown for old data, energetic red for crashes). Typography should be a playful, hand-lettered comic font. The overall feel is organic and friendly, not sleek or digital.

story_effectiveness.notes: Excellent. The four-panel comic perfectly illustrates the problem of data loss without WAL and the solution with WAL. The narrative flow from risk -> crash -> solution -> recovery is clear, logical, and easy to understand. The characters and their actions directly support the technical concept.

visual_consistency.notes: Flawless. The hand-drawn, colored pencil and watercolor style is beautifully consistent across all panels. The characters, labels, and overall layout (2x2 grid with wobbly borders) are perfectly maintained, creating a cohesive and charming visual experience.

pleasing.notes: Very pleasing. The art style is cute and engaging, making a technical subject approachable. The panels are well-composed and free of clutter, with clear visual hierarchy and a warm color palette.

critiques.revision_prompt: A four-panel technical comic in a playful, hand-drawn colored pencil and watercolor style on textured paper, explaining Write-Ahead Logging (WAL). The layout is a 2x2 grid with thick, wobbly borders. Top-Left Panel 'WITHOUT WAL: THE RISK': Anthropomorphic glowing 'RAM' character excitedly holds 'NEW DATA!' block; 'DISK-TREE' character holds 'OLD DATA' block. Top-Right Panel 'CRASH!': Large 'CRASH!' explosion icon; RAM is gone; Disk-tree looks sad with 'OLD DATA' block, saying 'I only know the old version.' Bottom-Left Panel 'WITH WAL: THE SOLUTION': Professor Inu Shiba points to a chalkboard flow: 'NEW WRITE' -> 'WAL (Log)' scroll -> 'RAM'. He says, 'Write is appended to log first.' Bottom-Right Panel 'AFTER RESTART': 'RESTART' icon; Professor Inu Shiba points to 'WAL' scroll showing 'NEW DATA!'; Disk-tree happily holds recovering 'NEW DATA!' block.
